description.stringValue: Learn more about the life of the Young family in Nauvoo and Brigham's leadership as the Saints prepared to leave for the west.

name.stringValue: Brigham and Mary Ann Young Home

description.stringValue: This large demonstration building showcases eight different arts and crafts from the early days of Nauvoo.
Visitors can research their ancestors inside the historic Hiram Clark store.
Hear the story of Heber and Vilate Kimball who followed prophetic leadership even when it was challenging.

name.stringValue: Hiram and Sarah Granger Kimball Home

description.stringValue: An important conversation in the Hiram and Sarah Granger Kimball Home in Nauvoo led to the birth of the all-female group known as the Relief Society.
The visitors' center has orientation services, visitor amenities, an exhibit, two theaters and administrative offices.
The visitors' center has orientation services, visitor amenities, an exhibit, a theater, and administrative offices.
Hold a group gathering in the barn near the home of Hiram and Sarah Granger Kimball.
In the Lucy Mack Smith Home, you will hear the story of a faithful woman who supported her son's calling as a prophet.
Learn about shopping and trade in Historic Nauvoo at the Lyon Drug Store, one of Nauvoo's largest dry goods stores.
The second home of Joseph and Emma Smith in Nauvoo. This house also served as a hotel for guests.
Jonathan Browning was an expert gunsmith who applied his trade and taught his family the gospel.
Since 1978, outdoor bronze statues commemorate the many ways women share their talents to bless the world.
The unfinished boarding house (or hotel) Joseph Smith was directed to build in an 1841 revelation.
Worship services for The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints are held every Sunday. Visitors are always welcome.
View the current temple information and appointment scheduling for the Nauvoo Illinois Temple.
Explore early Latter-day Saint mission history in the home of Orson and Marinda Hyde.
Experience musical theater performances in Nauvoo during the summer months.
Meet oxen while learning about the significance of these hardworking animals to the Latter-day Saints in Nauvoo.
Enjoy the summer performances of the Nauvoo and British pageants.
Enjoy a picnic or fire on the banks of the Mississippi River, the fire pit area at the west end of Parley Street.
Hold a group gathering in the shadow of the Nauvoo Temple in the wooded area on Partridge Street.

name.stringValue: Calvin and Sally Pendleton Home and Schoolhouse

description.stringValue: Learn about education in 1840s Nauvoo in the replica log home and schoolhouse of Calvin and Sally Pendleton.
Enjoy a picnic at the Partridge Street Wood.
Children of all ages can enjoy outdoor toys and games from the 1840s at the Pastimes Pavilion.

name.stringValue: Pioneer Memorial and Exodus to Greatness

description.stringValue: Remember those who left Nauvoo for Utah and those that died along the way.
Enjoy a picnic nearby the Pioneer Memorial.
Enjoy a picnic near the Pioneer Pastimes Pavilion.
The Pioneer Saints Cemetery is a quiet, contemplative cemetery with headstones dating from the 1840s.
Now rebuilt, this building was not only a general store, but also a civic and religious gathering place.
Learn more about George and Christiana Riser and how they contributed to the community in Nauvoo.

name.stringValue: George and Christiana Riser Home and Boot Shop

description.stringValue: Visit the bakery of Lucius and Lury Scovil and learn how they contributed to building of Nauvoo.
The reconstructed Seventies Hall in Historic Nauvoo allows visitors to learn about the gathering of the Saints for worship and instruction.
Hear stories about Nauvoo while riding on a horse-drawn carriage through the scenic landscape. Sleigh rides are available during winter months.
The burial place of Joseph and Emma Smith, Hyrum Smith, Joseph Smith Sr., Lucy Mack Smith, and several other members of the Smith family.
The first Nauvoo house of the Joseph and Emma Smith family was a place of protection, revelation, and healing.
In the Stoddard Tin Shop, take a look into the tin trade in Nauvoo.
Learn about the Taylor family and how they contributed to the community in Nauvoo.

name.stringValue: Taylor Home, Print Shop, and Post Office

description.stringValue: Temple patrons and other guests are welcome to use this building as a waiting or changing area.
The Temple Stones Pavilion shows how the Saints cut limestone to realize the vision of the Nauvoo Temple in the 1840s.
Learn the stories of Latter-Day Saint pioneers who made the difficult journey west to escape persecution.
Enjoy a picnic nearby the Visitor Center.
Meet a team of horses and take a ride through town as a missionary tour guide provides an overview of the physical setting of Nauvoo.
Learn of the important role the Webb brothers and their blacksmith shop played as the Saints prepared to move west.
A solemn site of remembrance, this restored jail honors the lives and legacy of the Prophet Joseph Smith and his brother Hyrum.
A grove of trees replanted near the Nauvoo temple helps tell the story of Latter-day Saint worship in the 1840s.
Learn of the Woodruff family's faith as they served and supported Church leaders—including Wilford—in Nauvoo.
Understand the process of designing the Nauvoo Temple in the home of William Weeks, the temple's architect, and his wife, Caroline.
Learn about the many important revelations Joseph Smith received about the eternal life of humanity in the restored home of William and Esther Gheen.
Guests who visit the historic jail begin their tour in the visitors' center where they view a short introductory film.
The Cultural Hall became a hub of the Latter-day Saints' spiritual, cultural, educational, economic, and civic activities.
Visit Patty Sessions's replica log home, and learn how she was an important midwife in the Nauvoo community.
In the home of Edward and Ann Hunter, learn how many Saints—like the Hunters—gave of their time and substance to help build the Nauvoo Temple.
